Refine Requirements · v3 Select any text to leave a comment
New Initiative classification 0.9

Replace the plain render with a maker that lets a reader grasp the job in seconds.

Time-to-understand

<60sA first-time reader states the job, outcome, and scope from the page alone.

In scope

WHAT + HOW maker pipeline Bespoke per family LLM quality gate Comment/version survival Cache by source hash Discoverable commenting Ask upstream for gaps

Out of scope

Replacing the markdown source / comment backend Human timed-read eval Rendering non-requirements docs A live render on every view
1

What this initiative is — a render that lands in seconds

The whole point is comprehension speed: replace v2's plain deterministic render with a cast-preso-style LLM maker so a reader grasps the job, outcome, and scope within seconds — and the above-the-fold view alone carries that summary, degrading a stub source to a clear prompt-to-begin state rather than an empty or broken page.

A reader instantly understands any render

US1User StoryP1
As a reader unfamiliar with a goal, I want to open its requirements render and grasp the job, the primary outcome, and what is in and out of scope within seconds, so that I can act without reading the whole document or asking the author. Independent test: Open the render for a goal in any family and, from the above-the-fold view alone, restate the job, the outcome, and the scope correctly. Acceptance scenarios: - Scenario 1: WHEN a reader opens a goal's render, THE SYSTEM SHALL present an above-the-fold summary (classification, one-line job statement, and the key outcome and scope assertions) that is readable without scrolling or clicking. - Scenario 2: WHEN the source document is a stub, IF there is not enough content to summarize, THE SYSTEM SHALL render a clear prompt-to-begin state rather than an empty or broken page.

Sixty-second cold read

SC-001Success Criterion
A first-time reader can state the job, the primary outcome, and the scope within sixty seconds of opening any family render.

Verified by: Cold-reader test (the cast-requirements-checker agent stands in for the human reader).

2

A bespoke render per classification, built by a WHAT planner and a HOW maker

The engine is a two-agent maker pipeline mirroring cast-preso's what-then-how split: a WHAT agent decides the information and outcome per block and family; a separate HOW maker chooses the representation and generates the self-contained HTML, never inventing the WHAT — picking from a named archetype library so every family renders a visibly distinct, appropriate page and empty blocks are omitted.

The layout is bespoke per classification

US2User StoryP1
As a reader, I want to see a layout shaped to the kind of work, so that a bug fix reads like a bug fix and a new initiative reads like an initiative. Independent test: Render one goal per family and confirm each family's layout is visibly distinct and appropriate to that family's recipe. Acceptance scenarios: - Scenario 1: WHEN the maker renders a document, THE SYSTEM SHALL select a layout and emphasis driven by the document's classified family and its recipe blocks. - Scenario 2: WHEN a family carries no content for a block, THE SYSTEM SHALL omit that block rather than pad it with an empty section.

The HOW maker generates the page

FR-001Functional Requirement
A HOW maker agent (a cast-requirements-how cousin of cast-preso-how) consumes the per-section communication intent produced by the WHAT agent plus the confirmed classification and produces the self-contained HTML render.

Notes: The HOW layer; replaces the single deterministic renderer on the happy path.

Bespoke per family

FR-002Functional Requirement
The maker selects layout and emphasis from the document's family and recipe blocks, producing a visibly distinct render per family.

Notes: Per-classification bespoke output.

The new agent set

FR-010Functional Requirement
The new agents are a WHAT agent, a HOW maker, a single thorough comprehension-plus-visual checker, and a diff-and-comment-resolution agent (plus helpers as needed), following the cast agent-design conventions.

Notes: The owner chose a WHAT-plus-HOW-plus-one-checker shape over a fuller multi-checker coordinator.

WHAT and HOW, split

FR-012Functional Requirement
The pipeline is split into two agents: a WHAT agent decides, per block and family, the information and outcome to communicate; a separate HOW maker chooses the representation and generates the HTML. The HOW layer never invents the WHAT.

Notes: Mirrors the cast-preso what-planner-then-how split, the structure that makes its output good.

A named archetype library

FR-013Functional Requirement
The maker selects representations from a named archetype library rather than improvising, reusing cast-preso archetypes (compare-contrast and its code-diff variant, single-stat-hero, timeline, diagram-annotated, and others) and cast-preso's diff-representation utilities for the version-diff surface.

Notes: Variety of representation forms; reuse over rebuild.

A distinct layout per family

SC-002Success Criterion
Every work family renders a visibly distinct and appropriate layout, confirmed across all nine families.

Verified by: One golden render per family, reviewed.

3

Comments and versions survive the switch to the maker

Switching to a varying maker render must not orphan a single reviewer comment. A deterministic id backbone emitted verbatim keeps the structural version diff as id-based set-arithmetic; an LLM reasons about meaning only to re-anchor or narrate text that genuinely moved or was reworded, never silently dropping a comment or inventing a change absent from the source.

Comments and versions survive the new render

US3User StoryP1
As a reviewer who has left comments, I want to keep my comments anchored after the render changes, so that switching to the maker does not orphan my feedback. Independent test: With open comments present, regenerate the render through the maker and confirm every comment stays anchored to its quoted text with zero new orphans. Acceptance scenarios: - Scenario 1: WHEN the maker emits a document, THE SYSTEM SHALL produce stable anchors for each user story, functional requirement, and success criterion so the comment and version layer can attach to them. - Scenario 2: WHEN a regenerated render moves or rewrites anchored text, THE SYSTEM SHALL re-anchor or resolve the affected comments by reasoning about meaning (an LLM resolution agent), rather than relying on the v2 deterministic quote-match alone, and SHALL never silently drop a comment.

Deterministic id backbone

FR-003Functional Requirement
Canonical anchor ids (US-NN, FR-NNN, SC-NNN) are assigned upstream by the deterministic engine, and the maker must emit them verbatim on the corresponding blocks; it never invents or renames an id. An LLM re-anchors a comment only when its target text genuinely moved or was reworded.

Notes: Deterministic id backbone; the maker decorates, it does not own identity.

Diffs stay deterministic

FR-011Functional Requirement
Because anchor ids are stable (the deterministic backbone above), the structural version diff stays deterministic (id-based set-arithmetic, as in v2); an LLM is used only to narrate the diff and to re-anchor or resolve comments whose text genuinely moved or was reworded, never inventing a change absent from the source.

Notes: The deterministic-backbone decision keeps diffs deterministic; the LLM handles only moved or reworded text.

Zero new comment orphans

SC-003Success Criterion
Regenerating a render leaves every open comment anchored, with zero orphans introduced by the render change.

Verified by: Comment-survival check across a regenerate.

4

Generated quality is gated, with a graceful fallback

A bad generation never reaches a reader: one comprehension-plus-visual checker grades the HTML and drives a quality-driven rework loop bounded only by a high safety ceiling, not a cost cap. On a true no-output crash the deterministic renderer is served; on non-convergence the best-scoring attempt is served and flagged for human review — never a downgrade to the plain page.

Generated quality is gated, not assumed

US4User StoryP2
As a maintainer, I want to gate the generated HTML behind a checker with a bounded rework loop, so that a bad generation never reaches a reader. Independent test: Force a low-quality generation and confirm the checker fails it and triggers a rework before the page is served. Acceptance scenarios: - Scenario 1: WHEN the maker produces a render, THE SYSTEM SHALL run a comprehension-and-visual checker over it and serve the result only after it passes or the rework budget is exhausted. - Scenario 2: IF the checker cannot reach the bar after sustained rework, THEN THE SYSTEM SHALL serve the best-scoring attempt and flag it for human review, rather than fall back to the plain deterministic page.

Quality-driven rework loop

FR-004Functional Requirement
A single new requirements-render checker agent grades the generated HTML for comprehension and visual quality and drives a rework loop that continues until the comprehension bar is met — quality-driven, not a cost-capped number of rounds (only a high safety ceiling guards against an infinite loop).

Notes: The page quality is the objective, so loops are not rationed.

Two-pronged fallback

FR-006Functional Requirement
On a true maker failure (crash, timeout, no output at all) the deterministic renderer is served. On non-convergence (output was produced but the checker never reached the bar) the best-scoring attempt is served and flagged for human review — never the plain deterministic page.

Notes: The reader always gets the best available page; the plain fallback is only for a no-output crash.

Verification layer revisited

FR-009Functional Requirement
The v2 verification layer is revisited for a non-deterministic render: the deterministic golden-HTML snapshot gate is replaced or supplemented by LLM-judged comprehension and visual checks.

Notes: New LLM checker agents are expected, not just reuse of the deterministic gate.

A render is never missing

SC-004Success Criterion
On a true maker crash or timeout (no output), the deterministic render is served, so a render is never missing.

Verified by: Fault-injection of the maker (no-output).

Best attempt, flagged

SC-008Success Criterion
On non-convergence, the reader is served the best-scoring attempt with a human-review flag recorded, never the plain deterministic page.

Verified by: Force the checker to never pass; confirm best-attempt is served and a flag is raised.

5

Generate once per source version, then cache

The expensive render is generated at most once per source version — an unchanged source serves cached HTML with no new model call. Caching reuses the v2 embedded-source-hash lazy-regeneration unchanged, regenerating on the next view when the source changes; it rations nothing, it only skips re-rendering identical content.

Renders are cached, not regenerated on every view

US5User StoryP2
As a cost-conscious owner, I want to generate the expensive render at most once per source version, so that viewing a page does not repeatedly call a model. Independent test: View an unchanged render twice and confirm the second view serves cached HTML with no new model call. Acceptance scenarios: - Scenario 1: WHEN a render is requested and the source content is unchanged since the last generation, THE SYSTEM SHALL serve the cached HTML without invoking the maker. - Scenario 2: WHEN the source content changes, THE SYSTEM SHALL regenerate on the next view and replace the cached render.

Cache by source hash

FR-005Functional Requirement
The generated render is cached against the source content hash and regenerated when the source changes, reusing the v2 embedded-source-hash lazy-regeneration mechanism unchanged.

Notes: Caching only skips re-rendering identical content; it does not ration generation effort.

Cache hit, no model call

SC-005Success Criterion
An unchanged source serves the cached render with no new model call.

Verified by: Cache-hit observation on a repeat view.

6

Commenting is discoverable, not hidden behind a gesture

A first-time reader can find how to comment without being told. The render adds a visible commenting affordance — a hint or control — alongside the existing select-to-comment pill. This dogfooding fix is independent of the maker and can ship ahead of the rest of v3.

Commenting is discoverable

US6User StoryP2
As a reviewer opening a render for the first time, I want to see an obvious way to start a comment, so that I can leave feedback without knowing a hidden select-text gesture. Independent test: A first-time reader, given no instructions, leaves a comment on the render. Note: this fix is independent of the maker and applies to today's deterministic render too, so it can ship ahead of the rest of v3. Acceptance scenarios: - Scenario 1: WHEN a reader views a render, THE SYSTEM SHALL show a visible commenting affordance (a hint or control) in addition to the select-to-comment gesture. - Scenario 2: WHEN a reader selects text, THE SYSTEM SHALL continue to offer the inline comment pill it offers today.

Discoverable commenting

FR-014Functional Requirement
The render exposes a discoverable way to comment — a visible affordance, not selection-only with no hint — while keeping the existing select-to-comment path.

Notes: Dogfooding finding: today the comment pill appears only on text selection, so the feature reads as absent.

Comment without being told

SC-006Success Criterion
A first-time reader discovers how to leave a comment without being told.

Verified by: Unprompted usability check on the render.

7

Filling comprehension gaps by asking upstream, never fabricating

When the file omits a detail that would materially help the reader, the page either supplies it — by asking the upstream WHAT or refine agent before finalizing — or marks the gap explicitly. The presenter never fabricates requirement content; anything it obtains is reconciled back to the canonical source so the page never shows content that exists nowhere else.

The presenter fills comprehension gaps by asking upstream

US7User StoryP1
As a reader, I want to get the detail I need to understand a requirement even when the source file left it out, so that I am never stuck on a page that explains something incompletely. Independent test: Given a requirements file missing a detail that materially aids comprehension, the rendered page either supplies it (sourced by asking the upstream agent) or clearly marks the gap — it never silently ships an incomplete explanation. Acceptance scenarios: - Scenario 1: WHEN the presenter detects a comprehension gap the requirements file does not cover, THE SYSTEM SHALL request the missing detail from the upstream WHAT or refine agent before finalizing the render. - Scenario 2: WHEN the upstream agent cannot supply the detail, THE SYSTEM SHALL surface the gap explicitly on the page rather than fabricate an answer.

Ask upstream, never fabricate

FR-015Functional Requirement
When the presenter finds a comprehension gap — a detail that would materially help the reader but is absent from the requirements file — it requests that detail from the upstream WHAT or refine agent before finalizing, rather than rendering an incomplete page; it never fabricates requirement content.

Notes: HOW asks WHAT; the page is the objective, the source stays the truth.

Reconcile obtained detail upstream

FR-016Functional Requirement
Detail obtained by such a request is reconciled upstream (written back to or flagged on the canonical requirements) so the page never shows requirement content that exists nowhere else.

Notes: No silent invention; the canonical source stays complete.

Gap supplied or marked

SC-007Success Criterion
When the requirements omit a detail needed to understand a block, the page supplies it (sourced upstream) or marks the gap, never shipping a silently incomplete explanation.

Verified by: Gap-injection test: remove a key detail and confirm the render asks upstream or flags it.

8

What deliberately stays the same

This initiative changes how the page is generated, not how it is reached or where truth lives. The render is still served through the existing self-healing render loop and stays a self-contained single file; the canonical markdown remains the source of truth, with the HTML a read-only projection the maker never writes.

Same self-healing render route

FR-007Functional Requirement
The render is served through the existing self-healing GET /goals/{slug}/render loop and remains self-contained.

Notes: No change to how humans reach the page.

Canonical source unchanged

FR-008Functional Requirement
The canonical source stays refined_requirements.collab.md; the HTML is a generated, read-only projection.

Notes: The maker never writes the canonical markdown.

Generated projection of refined_requirements.collab.md — read-only. Select any passage to leave a comment; the canonical markdown remains the source of truth.
